skill.name: design-to-code
skill.description: Converts Figma designs into React and TypeScript implementation prompts using coderio, local helper scripts, and staged design extraction.
skill.summary: Generate React implementation plans and component code from Figma designs.
skill.author: MigoXLab

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical matches, but most are false positives from license text, Markdown code fences, and words such as design or code. Confirmed risk remains medium because the skill uses a local Node helper that fetches Figma data with a user token, writes project files, and saves generated component code.

medium_findings.title: Figma Token Used by Network Fetch Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to pass a Figma URL and token to the helper script. The script parses the URL, calls coderio Figma helpers, downloads a thumbnail, and writes processed design data. This is expected behavior, but users must understand that a personal access token is handled by local script and dependency code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network workflow is explicit and semantically tied to Figma fetching. I found no evidence of unrelated exfiltration, so the risk is controlled but real.

medium_findings.title: Generated Data and Code Written to Workspace
medium_findings.description: The helper creates process, scripts, and asset directories, writes processed Figma data, saves protocol files, and stores generated component code under the project source tree. This is necessary for the skill, but it can overwrite or introduce files if inputs or generated protocol data are wrong.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and repeated across the helper workflow. The writes target expected project paths, so this is a moderate operational risk, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: AI-Generated Component Paths Influence Code Save Target
medium_findings.description: The save-code command reads generated output and protocol data, resolves a component path, and writes the resulting code. Because protocol content is derived from design data and model-generated files, users should review paths and code before saving into a project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The save target depends on protocol data and a resolver from coderio. I did not find evidence that it escapes the project, but the path is not hardcoded in this file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Blocks Trigger External Command Matches
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md detections mostly point to fenced shell examples and inline node commands that users intentionally run during the workflow. These are not hidden command execution inside the skill, but they should remain visible in marketplace risk labels.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation examples, not calls to a process execution API. They still matter because users are asked to run the commands manually.

low_findings.title: License and Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Apache license URLs are ordinary license references. The weak cryptography and Windows SAM detections are caused by words in license text, Markdown, or imported identifier names, not actual cryptographic or credential access code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows license prose and benign identifiers, with no crypto API or Windows credential database access. No prompt-injection text was found in the searched files.

low_findings.title: Template Literals Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Several script detections identify JavaScript template literals used for messages, prompts, paths, and errors. These are not Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses template strings inside JavaScript expressions and prompt text. I found no child_process, exec, spawn, or equivalent shell execution in the helper file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Request Path Handles User Token
dangerous_patterns.description: The fetch-figma command accepts a user-provided Figma token and passes it into coderio helpers that fetch document and image data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token handling path is direct and visible. The intended destination is Figma-related processing, with no evidence of unrelated endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper writes design artifacts and generated code into local workspace directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fs.writeFileSync and saveGeneratedCode calls are explicit. They appear required by the workflow but can affect the user's project.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose time translating detailed Figma layouts into working React components. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a staged coderio workflow for extracting design data and generating implementation-ready files.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through Figma token setup and design data fetching.
Creates prompts for structure extraction from a Figma thumbnail.
Builds component and props extraction tasks from processed design data.
Generates React and TypeScript component prompts for staged implementation.
Saves processed protocols, component lists, and generated code through a Node helper script.
Supports recovery by separating fetch, structure, props, task planning, and code save phases.
limitations: Requires a valid Figma personal access token and accessible design URL.
Depends on coderio helper behavior and local Node package installation.
Generated code still needs human review before production use.
Visual fidelity depends on thumbnail quality and accurate model interpretation.

use_cases.title: Convert Marketing Screens
use_cases.description: Turn approved landing page designs into React component tasks with assets and layout details.

use_cases.title: Prototype Product Interfaces
use_cases.description: Move product mockups into a Vite and TailwindCSS React project for faster iteration.

use_cases.title: Document Design Implementation
use_cases.description: Create staged prompts and protocol files that explain component structure before code generation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Conversion
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single Figma frame.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design-to-code workflow for this Figma design. Help me fetch the design data, generate the structure prompt, and list the next manual steps.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Component Props
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate use after the structure has been saved.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the saved protocol and thumbnail to extract props for each repeated component. Keep text and image references faithful to the Figma design.

prompt_templates.title: Generate React Components
prompt_templates.scenario: Code generation after tasks are listed.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the next React and TypeScript component from the current code prompt. Match spacing, typography, colors, and asset usage from the thumbnail.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Integrate Output
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced final pass before committing generated files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the generated components against the thumbnail and protocol. Identify visual mismatches, unsafe paths, missing assets, and integration changes needed in App.tsx.

output_examples.input: A user provides a Figma URL for a product hero screen.
output_examples.output: The assistant fetches design data, prepares a structure prompt, and asks the user to attach the thumbnail for visual grouping.

output_examples.input: A user has a saved protocol and wants component tasks.
output: Props extraction prompts are prepared for repeated elements.
Generation tasks are ordered from child components to parent frames.

output_examples.input: A generated component does not match the screenshot.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the protocol, thumbnail, asset list, and generated code instructions before recommending targeted layout fixes.

best_practices: Use a Figma token with the least access needed for the selected file.
Review generated protocol paths and component names before saving code.
Commit or back up the project before running commands that write files.
anti_patterns: Do not paste a broad personal access token into shared logs or prompts.
Do not save generated code without reviewing imports, paths, and visual output.
Do not skip the thumbnail attachment when extracting structure or props.

faq.question: Does this skill directly connect to Figma?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper script accepts a Figma URL and personal access token to fetch design data and images.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready React code?
faq.answer: It can generate implementation-ready React and TypeScript components, but developers should review and test the result.

faq.question: Can I use it with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is compatible with Codex, Claude, and Claude Code when the local scripts are available.

faq.question: Will it overwrite existing files?
faq.answer: It writes generated artifacts and component code into the workspace. Use version control before saving generated output.

faq.question: Why does the workflow use several phases?
faq.answer: The phases separate Figma fetching, structure extraction, props extraction, task planning, and code generation for easier review.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it handles tokens, network access, local file writes, and user-run commands.
